query: effect of cardiopulmonary bypass temperature on postoperative euthyroid sick syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiopulmonary Bypass -- Technique or Treatment -- Confirming Satisfactory Bypass
retrieved_docs.content: Temperature management Check the temperature of the blood returning to the patient to ensure the heat exchanger appropriately regulates it. The temperature should be maintained within a safe range to avoid hypo- or hyperthermia.

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Management for Enhanced Recovery After Cardiac Surgery (ERACS) -- Issues of Concern -- Intraoperative Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Normothermia should be maintained after cardiopulmonary bypass and during the initial intensive care period. Hyperthermia (core temperature above 37.9 degrees Celsius) has been associated with cognitive dysfunction, infection, and renal injury. [2] Persistent hypothermia (core temperature below 36 degrees Celsius) can lead to bleeding, infection, longer hospital stays, and mortality. [2] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Bispectral Index -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Hypothermia: Doi et al studied 12 patients undergoing hypothermia for a cardiopulmonary bypass (CPB) pump and noticed a wide variation in BIS values during the decrease in body temperature. [14] The BIS decreases by 1.12 units for each degree Celsius reduction in body temperature. Reduction in temperature results in a reduced cerebral metabolic rate for oxygen, reflected on the EEG by the progression to an isoelectric/burst suppression pattern dominated by isoelectric periods. Aortic cannulation causes EEG slowing. The onset of CPB produces a transient EEG depression, most likely due to perfusion of the brain with a crystalloid prime solution. EEG slowing that may persist into the post-CPB period is seen during decreased CPB flow rates with the aortic cross-clamp released. Reduced CPB flow rates requested by the surgeon during aortic cross-clamp release often correlate with EEG slowing that may persist into the post-CPB period. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiopulmonary Bypass -- Equipment -- Heat Exchanger
retrieved_docs.content: Heat exchangers in CPB circuits regulate the temperature of the blood. They allow for the controlled cooling and warming of blood, crucial for protecting organs and tissues during surgery. The exchanger typically consists of a series of metal plates or tubes through which blood and a temperature-controlled fluid (usually water) flow, allowing heat transfer between the 2.

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiopulmonary Bypass -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: CPB also includes mechanisms to adjust the blood's chemical composition and temperature via a heat exchanger, ensuring the patient's metabolic needs are met. Throughout the procedure, cardiotomy suckers collect and salvage any blood lost during surgery, minimizing the need for transfusions. Cardiac vents maintain optimal cardiac pressures and prevent heart enlargement. Once the surgical procedure is complete, the aortic cross-clamp is applied to isolate the heart from the rest of the circulation, enabling the surgeon to work on a bloodless field. The CPB machine's careful coordination of these functions ensures continuous perfusion of vital organs and protection of myocardial tissue during the surgery, maintaining patient stability and viability throughout the procedure. retrieved_docs.title: Cardiopulmonary Bypass -- Technique or Treatment -- Step 1: Restarting the Heart and Lungs
retrieved_docs.content: Rewarming Rewarming is essential to reestablish the metabolism of cardiac myocytes. Due to the physical properties of body fluids, this process takes longer (0.3-0.5 °C/min) than the cooling process (0.5-1.5 °C/min). Rewarming is achieved systemically via the heat exchanger and using a "bear hugger" to warm the lower extremities. Rewarming must not happen too quickly to avoid creating microbubbles (Boyle law) and not overheating to prevent the denaturation of plasma proteins.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypothermia -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiopulmonary bypass and ECMO are the most invasive but effective ways to rewarm a hemodynamically unstable patient.

retrieved_docs.title: Intermittent Exotropia -- Complications -- Anesthesia-related

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Fever -- History and Physical -- Immediate Fever
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant hyperthermia: high-grade fever (greater than 40 C), occurs shortly after inhalational anesthetics or muscle relaxant (e.g., halothane or succinylcholine), may have a family history of death after anesthesia. Laboratory studies will reveal metabolic acidosis and hypercalcemia. If not readily recognized, it can cause cardiac arrest. The treatment is intravenous dantrolene, 100% oxygen, correction of acidosis, cooling blankets, and watching for myoglobinuria.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypothermia (Nursing) -- Pearls and Other issues
retrieved_docs.content: ECMO and cardiopulmonary bypass are the most invasive but effective way to rewarming an unstable patient.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Heat Loss -- Clinical Significance -- Perioperative Hypothermia [22] [21]
retrieved_docs.content: Due to the previously mentioned consequences of hypothermia, precautions in the perioperative setting against heat loss are essential. These include temperature monitoring, prewarming, maintaining an OR temperature of lower than 24 degrees C during induction, patient positioning with extremities tucked when possible, and warming of IV fluids.

retrieved_docs.title: Cardioplegia -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Cardioplegia following aortic cross-clamping results in many physiological changes that may hinder the recovery of the myocardium. It is essential to understand the electrolyte abnormalities, myocardial stunning, pH imbalance that may occur secondary to cardioplegia administration. Before coming off cardiopulmonary bypass, electrolytes, temperature, glucose, pH, hemoglobin, and hematocrit must be within normal limits. Excessive physiological changes may result in myocardial stunning, arrhythmias, ischemic injury leading to the inability to wean from cardiopulmonary bypass. Prophylactic measures are taken to reduce the complications of cardioplegia, such as frequent blood sampling by the perfusionist and notifying the surgeon and anesthesiologist of derangements while treating abnormalities as they present.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1184
retrieved_docs.content: Heart valve operations (عمليات صمام القلب) encompass a broad category of surgical interventions on cardiac valves. Within this category, “Other operations on valves of heart” are classified as heart valve operations, and specific procedures such as aortic valve replacement (استبدال الصمام الأبهري), mitral valve replacement (استبدال الصمام التاجي), tricuspid valve repair (إعاضة الصمام الثلاثي الشرف), and pulmonary valve repair (إعاضة الصمام الرئوي) are identified as narrower subsets of these operations. The procedure “رأب الصمام القلبي” is also considered a type of heart valve operation. Additionally, more complex intrathoracic procedures on the aortic valve performed with cardiopulmonary bypass are mapped primarily to the broader group of other operations on heart valves. Clinically, both aortic valve replacement (استبدال الصمام الأبهري) and mitral valve replacement (استبدال الصمام التاجي) are associated with rheumatic fever (الحُمَّى الروماتيزمية), highlighting the relevance of rheumatic disease in the etiology and management of valve pathology. Understanding these hierarchical relationships and clinical associations aids clinicians in categorizing valve surgeries, selecting appropriate therapeutic strategies, and anticipating disease‑related complications.

Both aortic and mitral valve replacements are clinically associated with rheumatic fever, reflecting the need for valve replacement after rheumatic damage. The specific operation “Replacement, tricuspid valve, with cardiopulmonary bypass” is a narrower instance within heart valve procedures and is performed as a Clinical act of insertion. Complex intrathoracic surgeries on heart arteries and valves often require cardiopulmonary bypass supported by extracorporeal circulation auxiliary to open‑heart procedures. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 176
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic fever (الحُمَّى الروماتيزمية) is clinically associated with aortic valve replacement (استبدال الصمام الأبهري) and coronary valve replacement (استبدال الصمام التاجي), indicating that patients with a history of rheumatic fever may require these specific valve interventions. Heart valve operations (عمليات صمام القلب) constitute a broad procedural family that includes cardiac valvotomy, excision of heart valve (part of “HEART: EXCISIONS”), valve excision techniques, heart valve repair (رأب الصمام القلبي), and other operations on valves of the heart (Other operations on valves of the heart). Narrower procedures within this category are tricuspid valve replacement with cardiopulmonary bypass (Replacement, tricuspid valve, with cardiopulmonary bypass), transcatheter aortic valve implantation (زراعة الصمام الأبهري عبر القسطرة), pulmonary valve replacement (إعاضة الصمام الرئوي), tricuspid valve repair (إعاضة الصمام الثلاثي الشرف), aortic valve replacement (استبدال الصمام الأبهري), and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 1149
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic fever (الحُمَّى الروماتيزمية) can lead to severe valvular damage that frequently requires heart‑valve operations, including aortic valve replacement (استبدال الصمام الأبهري), mitral (coronary) valve replacement (استبدال الصمام التاجي), tricuspid valve replacement, pulmonary valve replacement, transcatheter aortic valve implantation, and other aortic or mitral valve surgeries. These interventions are performed within the cardiovascular system, which also serves as the primary anatomic focus for a broad range of diagnostic, monitoring, and therapeutic procedures. Electrocardiographic and Monitoring Procedures, a subcategory of Cardiovascular and Respiratory System Procedures, encompass blood‑pressure monitoring, cardiotachometer monitoring, cardiotachometry, pacemaker therapy monitoring, routine pulse monitoring, and Holter monitor monitoring (the latter linked to unstable angina). Acoustic evaluations such as intracardiac phonocardiograms, phonocardiograms with apex

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1933
retrieved_docs.content: Other intrathoracic procedures on the aortic valve performed with cardiopulmonary bypass are classified as other operations on valves of the heart and are supported by extracorporeal circulation auxiliary to open‑heart procedures. In a similar manner, other intrathoracic procedures on the arteries of the heart that use cardiopulmonary bypass also depend on extracorporeal circulation as an auxiliary to open‑heart surgery. Both groups of procedures employ cardiopulmonary bypass to provide systemic perfusion while the heart is operated on, linking them to the broader category of extracorporeal‑circulation‑assisted cardiac operations.

Beyond diagnostic applications, certain radioisotopes are employed therapeutically, for example in thrombolytic therapy. Therapeutic agents also encompass cardioplegic solutions (kardioplegické roztoky) and cycloplegics (cykloplegika). Cardioplegic solutions are a type of medicinal solution (lékové roztoky) intentionally inducing cardiac arrest (شلّ القلب) during cardiac surgery. Extracorporeal circulation auxiliary to open‑heart procedures is classified as شلّ القلب and serves as the source (other_mapped_from) for intrathoracic procedures performed with cardiopulmonary bypass, specifically “other intrathoracic procedures on aortic valve with cardiopulmonary bypass” and “other intrathoracic procedures on arteries of heart with cardiopulmonary bypass.” The aortic‑valve procedures map primarily to “other operations on valves of heart,” whereas the arterial procedures map to “other operations on vessels of heart,”

retrieved_docs.content: Aortic valve replacement (استبدال الصمام الأبهري) and coronary (mitral) valve replacement (استبدال الصمام التاجي) are performed for rheumatic‑fever‑induced valvular damage and belong to the broader set of heart‑valve operations (عمليات صمام القلب) that also includes tricuspid valve repair (إعاضة الصمام الثلاثي الشرف), pulmonary valve repair (إعاضة الصمام الرئوي), valve commissurotomy (رأب الصمام القلبي), cardiac valve repair, coronary artery bypass grafting (مجازة الشريان التاجي), percutaneous coronary intervention (تدخل للأوعية التاجية عن طريق الجلد), and transcatheter aortic valve implantation (زراعة الصمام الأبهري عبر القسطرة). These procedures are carried out on the cardiovascular system (kardiovaskulární systém) and are supported by extensive diagnostic, monitoring and therapeutic modalities. Electrocardiographic and monitoring procedures form a major subgroup of cardiovascular and respiratory system interventions. They encompass cardiotachometer monitoring, intracardiac

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 186
retrieved_docs.content: extracorporeal circulation auxiliary to open‑heart operations. Extracorporeal circulation, a component of cardiovascular surgical procedures, supports a variety of open‑heart interventions such as operations on the aortic valve (mapped to “Other operations on valves of heart”) and operations on the arteries of the heart (mapped to “Other operations on vessels of heart”), both of which are linked to the coronary vessels. Overall, the knowledge network links PTCA, coronary thrombolysis, and injection‑based reperfusion strategies to the therapeutic framework of cardiovascular surgery, highlighting the roles of cardioplegic solutions, intentional cardiac arrest, and extracorporeal circulation in enabling safe and effective coronary and intrathoracic interventions. Understanding these interrelationships is essential for optimizing patient care, anticipating complications such as post‑PTCA angina, and selecting appropriate therapeutic modalities within the spectrum of coronary and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1572
retrieved_docs.content: Extracorporeal circulation auxiliary to open heart procedures is classified as شلّ القلب, indicating that this supportive technique used during open‑heart surgery is categorized under the concept of cardiac arrest.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 942
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac valve procedures (عمليات صمام القلب) encompass a range of surgical interventions that address dysfunction of the heart’s semilunar and atrioventricular valves. Specific procedures classified under this broader category include aortic valve replacement (استبدال الصمام الأبهري), mitral valve replacement (استبدال الصمام التاجي), tricuspid valve repair (إعاضة الصمام الثلاثي الشرف), pulmonary valve repair (إعاضة الصمام الرئوي), and valve stenosis (رأب الصمام القلبي). Both aortic valve replacement and mitral valve replacement are clinically associated with rheumatic fever (الحُمَّى الروماتيزمية), reflecting the disease’s potential to cause severe valvular damage that often necessitates surgical replacement of the aortic and mitral valves. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians understand the etiologic role of rheumatic fever in valvular pathology and guides appropriate referral to specialized cardiac valve surgery for affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 599
retrieved_docs.content: Cardioplegic solutions (kardioplegické roztoky) are a specialized subset of medicinal solutions (lékové roztoky) that are used to intentionally induce cardiac arrest (شلّ القلب) during cardiac surgery, providing myocardial protection while the heart is stopped. Extracorporeal circulation auxiliary to open‑heart procedures, which includes the use of a heart‑lung machine, is closely linked to the management of cardiac arrest because it supplies oxygenated blood to the body while the heart is arrested by cardioplegic solutions. This extracorporeal support also underlies a range of other intrathoracic procedures performed with cardiopulmonary bypass. Procedures performed on the aortic valve with cardiopulmonary bypass are categorized as “other intrathoracic procedures on the aortic valve with cardiopulmonary bypass” and are mapped to the broader group of “other operations on valves of the heart.” Similarly, procedures on the coronary arteries performed with cardiopulmonary bypass are described as “other intrathoracic procedures on arteries of the heart with cardiopulmonary bypass” and are mapped to “other operations on vessels of the heart.” These vessel‑focused operations have a direct relationship with the coronary vessels (koronární cévy), emphasizing their relevance to coronary artery disease and revascularization strategies. Together, cardioplegic solutions, extracorporeal circulation, and the various intrathoracic procedures with cardiopulmonary bypass form an integrated framework for safely arresting the heart, maintaining systemic perfusion, and addressing both valvular and coronary pathologies during complex open‑heart surgery. Effective coordination of these elements is essential for optimal myocardial protection, accurate surgical intervention, and improved patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 2 -- Community 757
retrieved_docs.content: Other intrathoracic procedures on arteries of heart with cardiopulmonary bypass are primarily mapped to the broader category of other operations on vessels of heart, indicating that these bypass‑related intrathoracic surgeries are classified within the general group of heart vessel operations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2644
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic fever (الحُمَّى الروماتيزمية) is clinically associated with the need for aortic valve replacement (استبدال الصمام الأبهري) and coronary valve replacement (استبدال الصمام التاجي). The inflammatory damage caused by rheumatic fever can affect heart valves, leading to severe dysfunction of the aortic and coronary valves that may require surgical replacement. Recognizing this relationship enables clinicians to monitor patients with rheumatic fever for progressive valvular disease and to plan timely surgical intervention, thereby improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2393
retrieved_docs.content: Other intrathoracic procedures on arteries of the heart performed with cardiopulmonary bypass are classified as extracorporeal circulation auxiliary to open‑heart procedures, and this categorization is derived from the mapping indicated by other_mapped_from.
